description: The provided image is a placeholder 'Check for updates' badge and does not contain scientific data. However, based on the paper context describing low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-4.5Al-1.47Mn), this figure likely belongs to a series showing microstructural evolution across samples rolled at 650°C, 750°C, 850°C, and 950°C. The paper investigates ultra-fine spheroidized carbides (M3C, M23C6, M7C3, 158–247 nm) dispersed in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae and their effect on impact toughness. Potential figure content includes SEM micrographs of lamellar δ-ferrite and martensite structures, TEM images of carbide distributions, EBSD orientation maps showing texture transitions from <001> to <101>, or mechanical property curves comparing impact energies (207.5 J for R650 versus 5.6 J for R850). The text reports that low-temperature rolling produces carbide/ferrite lamellae with superior toughness through crack blunting and deflection mechanisms, while high-temperature rolling yields martensite/DIF structures with high hardness mismatch (>3 GPa) and brittle fracture.

Figure 1 illustrates the comprehensive experimental protocol and specimen geometries for a low-density δ-ferrite steel. Panel (a) presents the thermomechanical processing schedule: the vacuum-induction-melted ingot was first homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm thickness, and air cooled. Subsequently, specimens were reheated to four distinct temperatures (650°C, 750°C, 850°C, and 950°C), held for 2 h, subjected to hot rolling with a 60% reduction ratio (reducing thickness from 40 mm to 16 mm), and finally air cooled, yielding sample groups R650, R750, R850, and R950. The Ac1 critical temperature is indicated as a reference. Panel (b) shows the cylindrical tensile specimen geometry with a gauge length of 25 mm (along RD) and diameter of 5 mm. Panel (c) depicts the Charpy V-notch impact specimen dimensions (10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm) with the notch on the rolled surface and impact direction perpendicular to the rolling direction (RD), with the normal direction (ND) labeled. This figure establishes the processing window covering carbide precipitation/dissolution (≤750°C) and dynamic recrystallization/transformation (≥850°C), enabling systematic investigation of microstructure-mechanical property relationships.

Optical micrographs (OM) showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo low-density δ-ferrite steel processed at different rolling temperatures. Subfigures (a) R650 and (b) R750 represent low-temperature rolling at 650°C and 750°C, respectively, displaying a continuous banded lamellar structure consisting of alternating δ-ferrite lamellae (light regions) and carbide/ferrite (C/F) composite lamellae (dark regions). The proportion of C/F composite lamellae decreases from 51.5% to 43.3% as temperature increases from 650°C to 750°C. Subfigures (c) R850 and (d) R950 show high-temperature rolling at 850°C and 950°C, where carbides completely dissolve and the structure transforms into a duplex lamellar morphology comprising δ-ferrite lamellae (light) and honeycomb martensite lamellae (dark), with numerous equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains embedded within the martensite regions. The δ-ferrite fraction increases to 66.8% at 850°C then decreases to 41.0% at 950°C, while martensite fraction increases significantly. This microstructural transition from carbide-containing composite lamellae to hard martensite directly correlates with the dramatic reduction in impact toughness from 207.5 J (R650) to approximately 5 J (R850/R950), demonstrating that ultra-fine spheroidized carbides dispersed in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae are crucial for superior impact toughness through crack deflection and blunting mechanisms.

This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo) processed at varying rolling temperatures, utilizing both SEM and TEM imaging. Subfigures (a)-(c) display the as-forged control sample prior to rolling, revealing coarse δ-ferrite grains interspersed with martensite/austenite constituents without distinct lamellar features. Subfigures (d)-(f) for sample R650 (rolled at 650°C) demonstrate the formation of ultrafine spherical carbides (average diameter 158.3±76.9 nm, density 1.7±0.2 μm⁻²) dispersed within ferrite matrix, creating carbide/ferrite composite lamellae. Subfigures (g)-(i) for R750 (750°C) exhibit significant carbide coarsening (247.3±86.8 nm, density 0.6±0.1 μm⁻²) with a bimodal distribution characterized by coarse particles at grain boundaries and finer ones within grains, consistent with Ostwald ripening. Subfigures (j)-(l) and (m)-(o) for R850 and R950 respectively reveal complete carbide dissolution and transformation to honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite grains. The figure establishes that rolling temperature critically governs carbide evolution—from precipitation and spheroidization at low temperatures to dissolution at high temperatures—thereby dictating the resultant lamellar architecture and mechanical performance.

This figure presents a comprehensive EBSD characterization matrix of low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo) processed at different rolling temperatures, comprising sixteen sub-images arranged in a 4×4 grid. The rows represent samples R650, R750, R850, and R950 (rolled at 650°C, 750°C, 850°C, and 950°C respectively), while columns display distinct EBSD-derived maps: GB+GOS (grain boundary superimposed with grain orientation spread), KAM (kernel average misorientation), IPF (inverse pole figure), and NI (nanoindentation overlaid on IPF). Subfigures (a-d) show GB maps with green regions indicating GOS≤2° (recrystallized grains), where the high-temperature rolling zone (R850, R950) exhibits significantly higher proportions of GOS≤2° (19.4–25.8%) compared to the low-temperature zone (R650, R750) with 6.9–12.3%. Subfigures (e-h) display KAM maps revealing dislocation density evolution, with the caption stating low-temperature samples show higher KAM values (0.48–0.55°) versus high-temperature samples (0.3–0.4°), indicating reduced dislocation density at elevated rolling temperatures. Subfigures (i-l) present IPF maps showing texture evolution from predominant <001> orientation in low-temperature samples toward <101> in high-temperature samples, accompanied by grain refinement and increased high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) from 43.5–49.6% to 60.1–75.7%. Subfigures (m-p) combine nanoindentation hardness data with orientation maps, visually demonstrating the hardness distribution: low-temperature samples exhibit two distinct hardness levels (δ-ferrite lamellae at ~2.9 GPa and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae at ~3.6 GPa), while high-temperature samples display a three-tier hardness gradient (martensite 6.0–6.5 GPa > DIF ~2.8–3.0 GPa > δ-ferrite 2.6–2.8 GPa). The figure collectively demonstrates that increasing rolling temperature transforms the microstructure from a dislocation-rich, precipitation-strengthened lamellar structure to a partially recrystallized duplex structure with martensite and deformation-induced ferrite (DIF), critically influencing the local mechanical behavior and crack initiation resistance through interfacial hardness mismatch mechanisms.

This figure comprehensively presents the mechanical property evolution of low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo) processed at rolling temperatures of 650°C (R650), 750°C (R750), 850°C (R850), and 950°C (R950). Panel (a) shows room-temperature Charpy impact energy decreasing precipitously from 207.5 ± 29.3 J for R650 to 4.6 ± 0.2 J for R950 as rolling temperature increases. Panels (b) and (c) display impact load-displacement curves: R650 exhibits a pronounced toughness propagation stage with crack propagation energy (Ep) of 147.90 J and initiation energy (Ei) of 59.6 J, whereas R850 and R950 show steep single peaks with negligible Ei (~3.91 J) and Ep (~2.10 J), indicating unstable crack propagation. Panel (d) presents engineering stress-strain curves revealing that ultimate tensile strength increases from 868.7 MPa (R650) to 1254.5 MPa (R950) at the expense of elongation, which drops from 19.0% to 3.5%. Panels (e) and (f) illustrate work-hardening rate curves versus true strain; low-temperature rolled specimens (R650/R750) exhibit a characteristic "hump" shape attributed to the Orowan mechanism of carbide-dislocation interaction, while high-temperature specimens (R850/R950) show monotonic decay without strain hardening capacity. These data collectively demonstrate that ultra-fine spheroidized carbides dispersed in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae at lower rolling temperatures enable superior impact toughness through enhanced crack blunting and deflection mechanisms, whereas high-temperature processing produces martensite/DIF microstructures with severe hardness mismatch leading to brittle fracture.

Figure 6 presents thermodynamic phase stability data for a low-density δ-ferrite steel (Fe-0.35C-1.47Mn-4.5Al-1.47Ni-0.26Cr-0.28Mo wt.%). Panel (a) shows the iron-carbon equilibrium phase diagram with temperature (600–1400°C) versus carbon content (0–1.5 wt.%), delineating phase fields including BCC (ferrite), FCC (austenite), BCC+FCC, BCC+carbides, and BCC+FCC+θ, with a vertical dashed line near 0.35 wt.% C indicating the alloy composition. Panel (b) displays the calculated volume fraction of each phase as a function of temperature (550–1150°C), where the black curve represents carbides, red represents BCC, and blue represents FCC. The graphs demonstrate that carbides remain stable below approximately 720–750°C, while FCC austenite emerges above this temperature range as carbides dissolve. These calculations explain the experimental observations that low-temperature rolling (650–750°C) preserves ultra-fine spherical carbides within ferrite/carbide composite lamellae, whereas high-temperature rolling (>850°C) leads to complete carbide dissolution and formation of a duplex δ-ferrite/martensite structure, thereby validating the processing–microstructure relationship governing impact toughness.

This figure presents comprehensive transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of carbides in the R650 low-density δ-ferrite steel (rolled at 650 °C). Subfigures (a)-(d) display bright-field TEM micrographs alongside selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns identifying three distinct carbide phases: (a) general carbide overview showing ultra-fine spherical particles (scale bar 125 nm), (b) M3C carbide with [201] zone axis diffraction pattern, (c) M23C6 carbide with [110] zone axis pattern (scale bar 50 nm), and (d) M7C3 carbide with [1092] zone axis pattern (scale bar 50 nm). The accompanying energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping reveals Mn and Cr enrichment within the carbide particles, contrasted by Al depletion in these regions, confirming the alloy carbide nature (M3C, M23C6, M7C3 where M = Mn, Cr). These carbides, with average size ~158 nm, are distributed within the ferrite matrix of composite lamellae, originating from martensite decomposition during low-temperature rolling. The combined TEM-SAED-EDS evidence confirms that these ultra-fine spherical carbides precipitate in original martensite regions via element enrichment (C, Mn, Cr) after martensite fragmentation, supporting the mechanism of superior impact toughness through crack blunting and deflection at carbide/ferrite interfaces.

This figure presents optical microscopy (OM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the deformed zones in impact and tensile fracture surfaces for sample R650 (rolled at 650 °C), a low-density δ-ferrite steel containing ultra-fine spheroidized carbides. Subfigures (a–c) are OM images showing the lamellar microstructure with alternating δ-ferrite and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae, where cracks initiate and propagate. Subfigures (d–g) are SEM images of the impact fracture zone: (d) shows a significantly blunted crack tip (black arrow) arrested at carbide/ferrite lamellae; (e) identifies primary and secondary cracks; (f) reveals microvoids and microcracks (white arrows) nucleated at carbide/ferrite interfaces; and (g) displays interface separation (dashed lines) along the lamellae. Subfigures (h,i) are SEM images of the tensile fracture zone, demonstrating cracks confined within and propagating parallel to the δ-ferrite lamellae. The figure evidences that nanoscale spherical carbides (diameter <250 nm) dispersed in ferrite lamellae effectively blunt crack tips, increase the curvature radius to reduce stress concentration, and induce microvoid formation at interfaces, thereby deflecting cracks horizontally along lamellae rather than allowing vertical penetration. This crack arrest mechanism explains the superior impact toughness (207.5 J) and strength-plasticity synergy in the low-temperature rolled microstructure compared to high-temperature rolled counterparts.

Figure 9 presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the deformed zones in impact fracture surfaces and schematic diagrams illustrating the impact fracture mechanisms for low-density δ-ferrite steel samples processed at different rolling temperatures. Subfigures (a) and (c) are bright-field TEM images (scale bar 1 μm) showing the lamellar microstructure and crack paths in sample R650 (rolled at 650°C), with dashed circles marking specific deformation features. Subfigures (b) and (d) are high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (scale bar 5 nm) with corresponding inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) insets revealing atomic shear and dislocation arrangements at the nanoscale. Subfigures (e)-(g) schematically depict the microstructural evolution: (e) R650 with ultra-fine spheroidized carbides dispersed in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae and δ-ferrite; (f) R750 (750°C) with coarser carbides and reduced carbide density; (g) R850/R950 (850°C/950°C) showing a dual-phase structure of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains after complete carbide dissolution. Subfigures (h)-(j) illustrate the fracture behavior: (h) R650 exhibits crack deflection, blunting, and microvoid nucleation at carbide interfaces leading to high impact toughness (207.5 J); (i) R750 shows reduced crack deflection due to carbide coarsening; (j) R850/R950 demonstrate brittle cleavage fracture with crack propagation shortcuts through DIF grains and high stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch (martensite ~6.0-6.5 GPa vs δ-ferrite ~2.6-2.8 GPa), resulting in dramatically reduced impact energy (<10 J). The figure establishes that ultra-fine carbides provide superior toughening via crack arrest and energy dissipation mechanisms, while high-temperature processing degrades toughness through microstructural coarsening and brittle phase formation.
